explicit_text: Japan was my homeland where my father stayed and later fell ill. I returned there for an arranged marriage, and we were married at a Shinto shrine by a Shinto priest. Even though I had planned to educate my children in Japan, I ended up raising them in America since I had no relatives there to care for them. During World War II, I sent many desperately needed supplies to our relatives in Japan from America.
implicit_text: That island nation was my homeland where my father stayed and later fell ill. I returned there for an arranged marriage, and we were married at a Shinto shrine by a Shinto priest. Even though I had planned to educate my children there, I ended up raising them in America since I had no relatives there to care for them. During the global conflict from 1939 to 1945, I sent many desperately needed supplies to our relatives in that country from America.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in the United States, I stayed with my brother and sister and worked chopping wood. I lived mostly among Japanese people at first and had little contact with Caucasians except for business. After World War II began, my family moved from California to Utah, where I farmed and raised my children. I decided to settle in America after Japan was defeated because this was my children's country and life in Japan seemed difficult after the war.

I decided to settle in this country after my homeland was defeated because this was my children's country and life in my birth country seemed difficult after the war.

explicit_text: World War II was a difficult time for us, especially after the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor. We experienced hardships and had to move from California to Utah. During the war, I sent supplies to relatives in Japan and realized the importance of helping from America. The war also influenced my decision to settle permanently in the United States rather than return to Japan.

explicit_text: I was embarrassed by the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor, which I understood resulted from American pressure on Japan for oil. This event marked a turning point, leading to suspicion and distrust of Japanese people in America. It also triggered the relocation orders that affected me and many others.

explicit_text: As a Nisei, I faced many challenges during the relocation orders in World War II. I initially thought the notice applied only to Issei, but was disappointed to learn it included all Nisei. I sold my nursery in Seattle at a great loss and prepared for relocation. I believe Nisei would be in a better position if they knew the Japanese language, and I also felt it was unfair to draft Nisei while they were being evacuated and treated as enemies.
implicit_text: As a second-generation Japanese American, I faced many challenges during the relocation orders in the global conflict. I initially thought the notice applied only to first-generation immigrants, but was disappointed to learn it included all second-generation as well. I sold my nursery in a Pacific Northwest city at a great loss and prepared for relocation. I believe second-generation Japanese Americans would be in a better position if they knew the Japanese language, and I also felt it was unfair to draft them while they were being evacuated and treated as enemies.

explicit_text: I thought the relocation notice during World War II applied only to Issei, the first-generation Japanese immigrants, and was prepared to turn over my nursery to my eldest son. However, I was disappointed to find that the notice included all Nisei as well. This affected my plans and forced me to sell my nursery at a steep loss.

The pastor and church community supported me, though they too faced similar hardships.
